does: This paper conducts a large-scale empirical analysis of the AI alignment research landscape by collecting and analyzing existing alignment research articles and publications. The authors systematically identify emerging subfields, map prominent researchers and their contributions, extract recurring topics and themes, and analyze different modes of communication across the field. They develop a classifier trained on alignment research that can identify relevant articles not in their original dataset, demonstrating the utility of their analytical framework. The work produces a curated dataset of alignment research and uses unsupervised methods to characterize the structural organization and knowledge distribution within the field.
connects: This work builds on a growing recognition within AI safety that understanding research landscapes helps solve collective action problems in emerging fields. It extends prior efforts in bibliometric analysis and research mapping to the specialized domain of AI alignment, responding to the acknowledged opacity of a field that prioritizes rapid information exchange across fragmented communication channels. The classifier development draws on standard text classification and information retrieval methods. The paper connects to broader discussions in philosophy of science about how research communities form, what barriers prevent entry for newcomers, and how field fragmentation affects knowledge accumulation. It responds to practical concerns from alignment researchers about coordinating work across parallel subfields and institutions.
enables: This work establishes infrastructure for future systematic study of alignment research and enables several follow-up directions. The shared dataset can support longitudinal studies tracking how alignment subfields evolve and interact. The classifier provides a tool for automatically organizing new alignment papers as they emerge, maintaining a dynamic map of the research landscape. The identified topics and researcher networks can inform mentorship and collaboration strategies for newcomers entering the field. Future work could develop interactive visualization tools or recommendation systems to help researchers discover related work and identify research gaps. More broadly, the methodology demonstrates how unsupervised analysis can make opaque research fields more navigable, with potential applications to other emerging areas in AI safety and machine learning research where rapid communication outpaces systematic documentation.
